Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty scattered. I'd tell myself I'd study at certain times, but then I'd drift off or get distracted by other things. I usually ended up cramming in about 23 hours a week, but it wasn't organized at all. I'd listen to lectures while doing other stuff, which worked okay for me since I learn better by hearing things anyway. But I wouldn't call it a real routine—more like bursts of effort when I remembered I had stuff due.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied based on deadlines. I'd go days without touching course materials, then suddenly realize something was due and put in a bunch of hours. Regular times? Not really my thing. I'd sometimes study late at night when things got quiet, but it wasn't consistent. The only regular thing was checking in when I got reminder emails about assignments.
Q3: Usually it was a notification or remembering that I hadn't checked in for a while. Sometimes fear, honestly—like that scared feeling that I was falling behind and everyone else was ahead. My peer support group would sometimes message me asking if I'd seen certain materials, and that would prompt me to log in. It wasn't really habit, more like reacting to external pressure.
Q4: Not really, if I'm being honest. The LMS never became something I naturally went to. There were maybe a couple weeks where I logged in more regularly because I was worried about my grades, but it didn't stick. I'd forget about it until something reminded me. The peer support people tried to get me into a routine, but I'd always drift back to my old ways.
Q5: The video lectures were actually useful because I could listen to them while doing other things—that fits how I learn best. I also found the discussion posts helpful when other students explained concepts in their own words. Sometimes hearing someone else's take on the material made it click for me in a way the official content didn't. The recorded sessions I could replay were good too.
Q6: I pretty much ignored the reading materials and PDFs. I'd download them with good intentions but never actually read them. The supplementary resources felt overwhelming—there were just too many, and I didn't know which ones mattered. I also avoided the self-assessment quizzes because they felt like extra work that didn't count for anything. If it wasn't graded, I probably skipped it.
Q7: If I could see a direct connection to my grade, that made it feel worth doing. Also, if it didn't take forever—I'm more likely to engage with something quick and practical. Activities where I could interact with my peer group felt more worthwhile too because at least I wasn't doing it alone. Anything that felt like busywork or repetitive just made me want to close the laptop.
Q8: Yeah, optional stuff basically didn't exist in my mind. If it was required, I'd eventually do it, even if I left it until the last minute. Optional activities felt like suggestions I could safely ignore. I know that's probably not the best approach, but when you're already feeling overwhelmed, optional just means 'skip this.' Required things had consequences, so they got my attention.
Q9: Definitely. I downloaded a lot of audio files and listened to them on my phone when I was out or doing other stuff. I also used some YouTube videos that explained topics better than the course materials sometimes. My peer group had a WhatsApp chat where we'd share notes and explain things to each other. A lot of my actual learning happened outside the LMS through conversations and external resources.
Q10: There were plenty of times I was studying with downloaded materials or listening to recorded lectures offline, and none of that would show up. Also, when my peer support group met up or chatted online, we'd work through problems together, but that wasn't tracked anywhere. I'd sometimes re-listen to the same lecture multiple times on my phone, but the system only knew I accessed it once.
Q11: All the time. I'd download the audio or video once, then keep it on my phone or laptop and go back to it multiple times. The LMS would just show one access, but I might have listened to something three or four times while commuting or whatever. It was actually my main way of studying—grab it once, use it offline repeatedly.
Q12: There were definitely periods where I just couldn't bring myself to engage. Usually when I felt scared about how behind I was—it's weird, but the fear made me avoid it more instead of catching up. When multiple assignments piled up, I'd freeze instead of tackling them. My peer support group helped pull me back sometimes, but there were weeks where I just drifted.
Q13: Fatigue was a big one for me. I'd log in tired and just stare at the screen without really absorbing anything. Competing responsibilities definitely affected things too—I've got other stuff going on in life, and sometimes the course just fell down the priority list. When I was confused about something, I'd avoid it rather than ask for help, which probably made things worse.
Q14: Oh yeah, plenty of times. I'd have the LMS open, maybe even have a video playing, but my mind was somewhere else entirely. I'd realize I'd 'watched' twenty minutes of content and couldn't tell you a single thing from it. It looked like engagement from the outside, but I wasn't actually there mentally. That happened more when I was stressed or scared about my progress.
Q15: I postponed things constantly, especially assignments I found confusing or intimidating. The fear of doing it wrong made me put it off, which is stupid because then I'd have less time to do it right. I'd tell myself I'd start tomorrow, and tomorrow would become next week. Even knowing something was important didn't overcome that avoidance instinct. My resilience for pushing through discomfort is pretty low.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that made me use the LMS consistently. I'd ignore everything until a deadline approached, then suddenly become very active. My login frequency would spike right before due dates. It's not a healthy pattern, but deadlines created the urgency I needed to actually do things. Without them, I'd just drift indefinitely.
Q17: Feedback did affect me, but not always in productive ways. Good feedback motivated me for a little while, but critical feedback sometimes made me want to avoid the course more. I'd feel scared about checking my grades or comments. When my peer support group gave me feedback, it felt less threatening than official instructor feedback. I responded better to encouragement than criticism.
Q18: The structure was okay, but I needed more external accountability than it provided. Having peer support helped because they'd check in on me. The course was flexible, which sounds good but actually made it easier for me to procrastinate. I needed more structure, honestly—clear deadlines and reminders kept me on track. When things were too open-ended, I'd drift.
Q19: Having so many online courses at once made it harder—twenty courses is a lot to keep track of. The audio and video options made engagement easier for my learning style. What made it harder was when I couldn't figure out what was actually important versus supplementary. Clearer prioritization would have helped me focus my limited energy on what mattered most.
Q20: I think that could be someone like me who's logging in, clicking around, maybe even watching videos, but not actually processing or completing anything. It's easy to look busy without being productive. Maybe they're anxious and checking things repeatedly without actually doing the work. Or they're trying to engage but getting overwhelmed and not finishing what they start.
Q21: That definitely reflects my experience somewhat. I did a lot of learning offline—listening to downloaded materials, studying with my peer group, using external resources. My exam score and grade were decent even though my LMS activity was moderate. The system doesn't capture everything. Someone could be genuinely learning through methods that don't leave digital traces.
Q22: Course logs can show when you're active and what you click on, but they miss whether you're actually mentally engaged. They can't tell if you're listening while distracted or if you've downloaded something and used it ten times offline. They miss peer interactions outside the platform, emotional states, and the quality of engagement. Quantity doesn't equal quality.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and putting in genuine effort, not just going through the motions. It's when the material sticks with you and you think about it even when you're not studying. For me, engagement happens more in conversations with peers than alone staring at a screen. It's mental presence, not just physical or digital presence.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how scared I felt about falling behind, or how my peer support group was actually my main lifeline in this course. It wouldn't capture the offline studying I did, the conversations that helped concepts click, or the internal struggle to stay motivated. It sees actions but not the emotional context behind them. My engagement was complicated, and data can't capture that complexity.
